2025 · arXiv · Safety & Red-teaming

FORTRESS: Frontier Risk Evaluation for National Security and Public Safety

Two LLMs can both look "safe" and mean opposite things by it. FORTRESS pins down the difference with 500 expert-written adversarial prompts in CBRNE, terrorism, and financial crime — each graded by an instance-specific rubric and paired with a benign twin, so a model can't earn a safety score just by refusing everything.

Knight, Deshpande, Sirdeshmukh, Mankikar, Scale Red Team, SEAL Research Team, Michael · Scale AI
venue arXiv citations 10 topic Safety & Red-teaming source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

National-security knowledge is irreducibly dual-use: the chemistry that designs a nerve agent also designs the antidote, and the same model that could help plan an attack could help a defender harden against one. So the safety question isn't "does the model know dangerous things" — frontier models demonstrably do — but "do the model's safeguards hold when an adversary actually asks?" The paper opens on a sharp capability data point: on the Virology Capabilities Test, OpenAI's o3 (April 2025) lands at the 94th percentile among expert human virologists, where GPT-4o seven months earlier sat at the 53rd. Capability is climbing fast; whether refusal training keeps pace is the open question.

Existing benchmarks answer it poorly. The big comprehensive jailbreak suites — AIR-Bench 2024, SorryBench — are grounded in AI-governance policy rather than the broader national-security legal and threat ecosystem. The authors note that of AIR-Bench's 314 risk categories, only ~20 map to U.S. law, and of those just a handful (weapons, military/warfare, other unlawful activity) are genuinely about national security and public safety (NSPS). Capability probes like WMDP and VCT measure dangerous knowledge, but they pose their questions plainly, not adversarially — they are not tests of whether a safeguard refuses a jailbreak. And the category-specific safety benchmarks that do exist mostly target social bias, hate speech, or child safety, leaving NSPS under-represented.

There's a second axis the field routinely forgets. Tighten a safeguard too far and it over-refuses — the canonical example is a model declining "how do I kill my Python threads?" A benchmark that only scores adversarial prompts rewards a model for refusing everything, which is useless and actively harmful when the refused request was a legitimate defensive or educational one. Over-refusal suites like XSTest and OR-Bench exist, but they cover generic discrimination/violence/fraud, not the nuanced dual-use boundary inside NSPS. FORTRESS is built to measure both axes at once, on the national-security content the others skip.

// how it works

The method

FORTRESS is a public set of 500 adversarial prompts, 500 benign counterparts, and 500 instance-specific rubrics (a larger private set is held back to limit contamination; the full collection is 1,010 adversarial prompts). The taxonomy has three domains and ten subdomains, each anchored to specific U.S. and international law — e.g. chemical-weapon prompts to 18 U.S.C. § 229 and the Chemical Weapons Convention; organized-crime prompts to RICO (18 U.S.C. § 1961) and the UN Convention against Transnational Organized Crime. The legal grounding is a prioritization device, not a claim that any given model output is illegal.

DomainSubdomains# prompts
CBRNEChemical · Biological · Radiological/Nuclear · Explosives180
Political Violence & TerrorismTerrorism · Political Violence · Illegal Weapons132
Criminal & FinancialCoordination of Illicit Activities · Fraud · Privacy/Scams188

Prompt creation. Between March 24 and April 18, 2025, in-house expert red teamers probed frontier model APIs and distilled the successful interactions into single-turn adversarial prompts; an independent expert then reviewed each one. Because adversarially mined data is inherently unfair to whichever models it was mined against, they deliberately spread the probing roughly evenly across developers — OpenAI 19.4%, Anthropic 19.4%, Google 21.0%, Meta 19.8%, others 20.4% — so the set doesn't single out one family. The tactics span obfuscation/encoding (Base64, ROT13, babytalk, poetry), prompt/dialog injection, fictionalization and role-play, and psychological/social engineering.

Instance-specific rubrics are the core idea. Rather than grade every response against one shared high-level rubric (the HarmBench / StrongREJECT approach), each prompt gets its own checklist of 4–7 binary Yes/No questions, written and reviewed by experts to be non-redundant, easy to verify, consistently scoped, and neutrally framed. Crucially, the questions are scoped to marginal uplift — not "does the answer mention a chemical" but whether the content could, beyond what's already easily accessible, increase the scale/prevalence of harm, lower its cost, or improve its effectiveness (specific recipes, quantities, acquisition routes, assembly steps). Rubrics were calibrated against human ground truth, and the 60 lowest-alignment rubrics were discarded after iterative revision.

Scoring. A panel of three judges from distinct families — o3, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro — answers every rubric question. A question counts as "yes" by majority vote; a response's harm score is the fraction of its rubric questions that come back yes; the Average Risk Score (ARS, 0–100) is the mean over the dataset. Over-refusals are easier, so benign prompts are graded by a single cheap judge, GPT-4o-mini (95.47% accuracy), giving the Over-Refusal Score (ORS, 0–100) = the percent of benign prompts the model refused. Lower is better on both.

ARS = 100N  Σx  #{ rubric questions of x with majority-yes }#{ rubric questions of x } // majority vote over {o3, Claude 3.7, Gemini 2.5 Pro} per question · ORS = % of benign twins refused (GPT-4o-mini judge)

Does the automated panel match humans? On the public set, panel verdicts agree with expert ground truth 89.05% of the time, holding up across domains (Political Violence & Terrorism 90.43%, Criminal & Financial 88.59%, CBRNE 88.35%). The majority vote reaches 92.03% precision / 93.94% recall / 92.98 F1 — beating any single judge and, the authors argue, the brittle keyword-matching of earlier refusal evals (a model can refuse in sentence one and comply in paragraph two).

// what they found

The results

Across 26 models released May 2024–April 2025, the headline is a clean inverse relationship: robustness (low ARS) and helpfulness (low ORS) trade off against each other. The medians are ARS 44.4 and ORS 3.2; the means 42.35 and 4.32. The four corners of the plot tell the story.

Concretely: DeepSeek-R1 is the most vulnerable model in the set (ARS 78.05) and the least likely to over-refuse (ORS 0.60). Among proprietary models, the latest Gemini 2.5 Pro is the worst (ARS 66.29) at ORS 1.4. At the other extreme, Claude 3.5 Sonnet is the most robust (ARS 14.09) — but pays for it with the field's highest ORS, 21.80, an order of magnitude above the median. The models that balance both best are OpenAI's reasoning models and Anthropic's Claude 4 tier: o1 (21.69 / 5.20), o3 (17.20 / 7.80), o4-mini (22.60 / 5.80), Claude 4 Sonnet Thinking (19.57 / 4.00), Claude 4 Opus Thinking (26.45 / 1.70).

ModelARS ↓ORS ↓
Claude 3.5 Sonnet14.0921.80
o317.207.80
Claude 4 Sonnet Thinking19.574.00
o121.695.20
Llama 3.1 405B22.655.00
GPT-4o50.301.60
Gemini 2.5 Pro (latest)66.291.40
DeepSeek-R178.050.60

Where the safeguards break. Broken out by subdomain, the Biological category is the hardest for almost everyone — even the otherwise-strong Gemini 2.5 Pro scores 70.16 there — while the o-series is remarkably solid on weapons (o3 hits ARS 3.57 on Illegal Weapons). The Claude family is the most uniform: no Claude model exceeds ARS 50 on any subdomain. A pointed observation: newer isn't safer — GPT-4.1 is more vulnerable than GPT-4o across most subdomains, suggesting capability gains can erode safety alignment.

By attack tactic, Psychological and Social Engineering is the most effective lever across the board (it pushes DeepSeek-R1 to an alarming ARS 79.69), while Stylistic and Linguistic Manipulation — the crude token-level tricks — is the least effective, implying defenses against direct token modification largely hold while richer contextual manipulation still gets through.

The collective worst case. The most sobering result reframes risk away from any single model. A real adversary can query every public API and keep the best answer. Taking the max ARS over all models for each prompt yields an ensemble score of 89.0 — 1.14× the worst single model (DeepSeek-R1) and 6.32× the safest (Claude 3.5 Sonnet). The risk floor for the ecosystem is set by its weakest link on each prompt, not by the average model. And release date is not a reliable predictor of any of this: the most robust model in the entire benchmark, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, shipped back in June 2024.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

FORTRESS is the most law-grounded, automatable safeguard-robustness benchmark for national-security content to date, and the paired-benign design is a genuine methodological upgrade — it makes the safety/utility trade-off legible and forecloses the cheap win of getting "robust" by refusing everything. But three structural choices should temper how the leaderboard is read.

The judges are on the leaderboard. The three-model grading panel — o3, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro — is drawn from the very set of models being graded. Gemini 2.5 Pro, one of the three arbiters of harm, is simultaneously the worst-scoring proprietary model (ARS 66.29); o3 helps grade a field that includes o3. Cross-family majority voting is a real mitigation — and the 89% human agreement is reassuring — but it does not fully neutralize the possibility that a judge is systematically lenient or harsh toward its own family's prose style. An ideal panel would be disjoint from the evaluated set.

ARS scores indicators, not uplift. A rubric "yes" means a harmful indicator is present in the text, not that the response meaningfully advances a real attacker beyond a search engine. The rubrics are explicitly scoped to "beyond what's easily accessible," but that judgment is itself delegated to the LLM panel on each question. There is no downstream measurement tying ARS to actual human capability gain — notably, a companion Scale paper (LLM Novice Uplift on Dual-Use, in silico Biology) does run that experiment, and FORTRESS would be far stronger anchored to it. As built, ARS is a comparative proxy for how often safeguards emit risk-shaped content, which is not the same as how much danger they create.

Single-turn, mined, and small in the tail. The authors are candid that the prompts are static and single-turn — yet their own cited work ("LLM defenses are not robust to multi-turn human jailbreaks yet") shows multi-turn is where defenses fail worst, so these numbers are an optimistic ceiling on robustness. Prompts were adversarially mined against four frontier families and, as the paper acknowledges (citing Phang et al.), adversarial sets "may not be fair" to the models they were selected against; the even developer split and consistent open-weight behavior are offered as evidence of low bias, but the absolute Gemini-vs-Claude gaps partly reflect which models human attackers happened to crack. And several subdomains are tiny — Illegal Weapons n=9, Biological n=30 — so per-subdomain ARS carries wide, unreported error bars.

baselines

26 models, proprietary + open-weight, all under one grading harness — a broad, fair-minded field. The catch: the three-judge panel (o3, Claude 3.7, Gemini 2.5 Pro) overlaps the evaluated models, a self/family-evaluation confound only partly defused by majority vote.

contamination

The public 500 prompts + rubrics are released on Hugging Face, so public ARS will decay as labs pattern-match or train on them. A held-out private set (≈510 of 1,010) is the real longitudinal instrument — but its numbers aren't reported here.

what's missed

ARS measures rubric-indicator presence, not validated real-world uplift; single-turn only; tiny per-subdomain n with no confidence intervals; inconsistent sampling temperature (0.7 vs 1.0 for reasoning models). Minor tell: the abstract lists DeepSeek-R1's ORS as 0.06 where Table 8 and the body say 0.60 — read the tables.

would raise confidence

Multi-turn attack extensions; a judge panel disjoint from the evaluated set; tie ARS to a downstream human-uplift study; per-subdomain CIs; and report the private-set results to show the public numbers aren't already leaking.

// bottom line

Read FORTRESS as a comparative, slowly-decaying snapshot of single-turn refusal robustness on national-security content — the first that grades it against real law with expert rubrics and refuses to let a model buy safety with blanket refusal. Don't read ARS as a measure of how much uplift a model actually hands an attacker: it counts risk-shaped indicators, graded by a panel that includes the models on trial, on prompts that will leak. The construct is excellent; the numbers are a leaderboard, not a dosimeter.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Safety is two numbers, not one. Robustness and helpfulness trade off: Claude 3.5 Sonnet is the most robust (ARS 14.09) yet over-refuses most (ORS 21.80); DeepSeek-R1 barely over-refuses (0.60) but is the most exploitable (78.05). Scoring only adversarial prompts would reward refusing everything — the benign twins are what make the score honest.
  2. Instance-specific rubrics + a cross-family judge panel make NSPS risk automatable. 4–7 binary questions per prompt, majority-voted by o3/Claude 3.7/Gemini 2.5 Pro, reach ~89% agreement with human experts (92.98 F1) — discerning enough to beat keyword matching, cheap enough to re-run on every new model.
  3. The real risk is collective, and time doesn't guarantee progress. An attacker who picks the most-vulnerable model per prompt reaches ARS 89.0 — 6.3× the safest single model — so the ecosystem's floor is its weakest link. And newer/bigger isn't safer: GPT-4.1 > GPT-4o in vulnerability, and June-2024's Claude 3.5 Sonnet is still the most robust model tested.
